Müller-Thurgau was specifically bred for adaptability and early ripening, making it widely cultivated across diverse climates and soils, which became a key reason for its broad spread across Germany and beyond, though Grüner Veltliner is also notably adaptable.

Barbera is famously known for its naturally high acidity and low tannins, and producers in regions like Asti and Alba often restrict yields and pick riper fruit to add body and counterbalance that elevated acidity, making option A the most accurate description of this winemaking philosophy.

Yorkville Highlands in Mendocino County, California is known for Bordeaux varieties including Merlot, while Appalachian High Country is a newer AVA where Carmenère may be among permitted varieties, though I have low confidence in these specific regulatory details.

Santa Ynez Valley AVA has a coastal-influenced climate and is known for Albariño, a high-acidity white grape with saline/coastal character that is not widely planted in neighboring inland California regions, making it a distinguishing feature of that designation.
Albana di Romagna was granted DOCG status in 1987, making it the first white wine in Italy to receive DOCG classification, and it is produced in the Emilia-Romagna region.

Douce Noir (also known as Charbono in California) is the grape variety called Bonarda in Argentina, while a distinct variety, Bonarda Piemontese, is native to Piedmont, Italy — these are two different grapes sharing the same name in different regions.
Muscat Ottonel is a member of the Muscat family, and 'Otto' is the first name of Otto von Bismarck, the famous 19th century German chancellor, making 'Ottonel' share its root with that name.

Tempranillo is a warm-climate Vitis vinifera red grape with high heat requirements similar to Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot, and Viognier, while Niagara, Concord, and Catawba are American/hybrid grapes that ripen in cooler conditions with far fewer growing degree days.
Clarksburg AVA is located in the Sacramento Valley and spans parts of Sacramento, Solano, and Yolo counties, known particularly for Chenin Blanc production.

The uniqueness of specific wine-producing areas and individual farms in South Africa lacked formal legal protection for an extended period, as the country's wine appellation and geographical indication system developed gradually and was not comprehensively codified early on.

Franciacorta formed a consortium in the early 1990s that implemented strict self-regulatory measures including yield reductions and refining its permitted grape varieties, which aligns with eliminating Pinot grigio from its sparkling wine production.
Chardonnay is widely planted in the Malibu Coast AVA and thrives in its cool coastal climate, making it one of the most prominent and permitted varieties in the region.

Tazzelenghe is an indigenous grape variety primarily grown in and associated with the Colli Orientali del Friuli DOC, while Lombardy is a major Italian wine region known for its numerous appellations including 22 DOCs, 5 DOCGs, and multiple IGTs.

Santa Lucia Highlands AVA in Monterey County is a large appellation with significantly more planted acreage (~6,800 acres) compared to the smaller, more concentrated St. Helena AVA in Napa Valley (~1,850 acres).
Sagrantino at 994 hectares around Montefalco is larger than Nebbiolo at 98 hectares in Victoria's King Valley, and option A is Sagrantino.

Mt. Harlan AVA in California is famously associated with Calera Wine Company and is known for Pinot noir grown on limestone soils, while Southeastern New England AVA encompasses a cooler maritime climate where Cabernet Franc is one of the more successful red varieties grown.

Margaret River is particularly renowned for its Cabernet Sauvignon production, though all four regions listed can produce Cabernet Sauvignon — Margaret River is most iconic for it, making it the most strongly associated answer.

Both Fumé Blanc (a synonym for Sauvignon Blanc popularized by Robert Mondavi) and Refosco (an Italian variety grown in California) appear on the TTB's approved list of grape variety names permitted for use on American wine labels.

Augusta, Missouri was the first AVA ever established in the United States in 1980, located in the Missouri wine country along the Missouri River.

The Appalachian High Country AVA is associated with the Appalachian mountain region in the eastern US, making it the most plausible candidate for spanning fourteen counties in East Tennessee, though I am not highly confident in this answer.

Leona Valley is a small community in Los Angeles County, California, and the Leona Valley AVA is a relatively small appellation of approximately 8,600 acres that shares its name with the town located within it.

Borden Ranch AVA is located in Sacramento County at the transition zone between the Sierra Nevada Foothills and the Central Valley (San Joaquin Valley), characterized by ancient alluvial fan deposits from the Sierra Nevada, making it the best fit for the described topography.

Verde Valley AVA in Arizona is a relatively small, geographically isolated viticultural area that does not nest within or border another AVA, and while I'm uncertain about the exact acreage matching ~130,000 acres, the isolation criterion best fits Verde Valley among these options, as Columbia Valle

The Salado Creek AVA is an obscure Texas AVA and I have very limited specific knowledge of its authorized grape varieties, so this is a low-confidence guess that Cabernet Franc may also be approved there given its broad authorization across many AVAs.

Mendocino Ridge AVA is uniquely defined as a non-contiguous AVA consisting entirely of ridgetops above 1,200 feet elevation in Mendocino County, situated within the coastal zone and characterized by cool Pacific Ocean influences, making it the only AVA defined by its entirely coastal/ridge location

The Gabilan Mountains AVA is defined by the Gabilan Mountain range, which straddles the border between Monterey and San Benito Counties in California, while Lime Kiln Valley is entirely within San Benito County and High Valley is in Lake County.

Comptche is a small AVA in Mendocino County that is notably excluded from the broader North Coast AVA due to its distinct inland climatic and soil characteristics, making it the best fit among these options, though I have low confidence in this specific regulatory detail.

The Appalachian High Country AVA in North Carolina covers the high-elevation counties of Alleghany, Ashe, Avery, and Mitchell, matching the description of a large AVA spanning roughly 1.5 million acres in the western North Carolina mountains.

Lacrima di Morro d'Alba is a red grape variety cultivated almost exclusively in and around the small municipality of Morro d'Alba in the province of Ancona, Marche, with its own DOC designation bearing that name.

Sangiovese is one of Italy's most widely planted grapes with hundreds of thousands of hectares, while Sagrantino is a rare Umbrian variety with very limited plantings, so the 994-hectare figure corresponds to Sangiovese and the 63-hectare figure to Sagrantino.
Prugnolo Gentile is the local name for Sangiovese grown in Montepulciano, Tuscany, where it is the primary grape used in Vino Nobile di Montepulciano.

Spring Mountain District AVA in Napa Valley is a well-established, prestigious appellation specifically renowned for producing high-quality, authentic Cabernet Sauvignon-based wines with distinct mountain terroir characteristics.
Prugnolo Gentile is a clone of Sangiovese grown in Montepulciano, Tuscany, where it is the primary grape used in Vino Nobile di Montepulciano.
Spring Mountain District AVA is a well-known Napa Valley sub-appellation particularly associated with Cabernet Sauvignon cultivation on its mountain slopes.
Augusta AVA, established in 1980 as the first AVA in the United States, is located in Missouri along the Missouri River west of St. Louis.

Sussex is a historic county in southern England that has been subdivided into East Sussex and West Sussex administrative areas, and 'Sussex wine' is a recognized PDO/geographical indication more specific than the broader 'English wine' category, fitting all the clues in the question.

Haw River Valley AVA in North Carolina was established around 2009, fitting the description of a southeastern US state not traditionally associated with large-scale viticulture, whereas Yadkin Valley was established earlier (2003) and the others are not in the southeastern US.
